clean_text: Alhamdulillah. Continuing in chapter number 73 verses number 8 9 and 10. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says to the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam after commanding him to stand in prayer specifically at night and this was the first obligation of prayer in Islamic legislation. He was to stand in salah in the nighttime recite the Quran at a reasonable pace. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala then mentions the reasoning behind the nighttime as opposed to the daytime and that is that the nighttime that it is more likely for the heart and tongue to be aligned and it is less likely for a person to make mistakes in his recitation at night due due to the lack of disturbance. He says subhanahu that in the daytime you have many chores to tend to errands etc. And so it becomes a less appropriate time for this very intimate interaction and meditation. He then says in three verses here, "And remember and recite the name of your Lord and cut yourself off to him." Now what's interesting about this verse is that if we were to take this verse in the context of what came before which is standing in prayer recitation reciting the Quran then here is as many of the scholars of have mentioned meaning that at the start of your recitation you begin with remember the name of your Lordis in the context of recitation. How do we remember the name of our Lord? Sayman at the beginning of our recitation. For this reason it's mentioned that when a person recites a chapter from its beginning then a person says in salah and outside of salah. However, if a person recites a part of the Quran from the middle or the end anywhere from after the first verse in salah a person doesn't say and the reason for that is that when we begin our prayer and we begin recitation of when we say this one time is sufficient for all of the prayer. Just when we say once at the beginning of the prayer and we don't repeat it again. However, outside of salah, if a person wishes to read any part of the Quran, not starting at the very first verse of a chapter, the correct opinion is that the person has the the choice to either begin his recitation withman or to say and begin reciting. This here is with respect to recitation. You for lack. And this is something that is common. You hear people that if a person for instance the and the question here is there's a difference between recitation of the Quran and mention of the Quran. If a person wishes to recite the Quran, these rulings are what apply to that. However, if a person merely wishes to mention the Quran, mention a verse, mention a cluster of verses to in a lecture or in aba or in a talk or in his teaching of his children or speaking a person wishes to mention a verse from the Quran. In this case, does a person say or does a person say ahead of the verse that he wishes to make mention of. The answer is no. You'll never find this anywhere that this is how the companions or the prophet sallall.aii was used to speak. And you hear this often wasch. So you hear this often. A person says you know his person is speaking and then they wish to mention a verse they say as Allah said after or a person says and then mentions a verse. You'll never find a narration this ever. Many many narrations in which the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam would mention a verse, mention a cluster of verses and he would not say nor would he say at the beginning before mentioning a verse. Likewise, You call it you read. for and so reciting as a person mentions a verse. This also is something that we wouldn't have any evidence for in the Quran and Sunnah. Rather the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam when he would go to mention a verse whether he's given a or speaking and he would say you know as Allah says in such and such verse he wouldn't recite it as he would in prayer he would just say the verse as it is. Say it as he would any words. This is how the companions spoke. They would speak the words of the Quran if they were speaking. However, in recitation, that is what these rulings are for. summary: This lecture analyzes Quran chapter 73, verses 8-10, focusing on the command to remember Allah's name and dedicate oneself to Him. The speaker discusses the proper etiquette of Quranic recitation, explaining the distinction between formal Quranic recitation (where Basmala rules apply) and merely mentioning Quranic verses in speech or teaching. A significant portion addresses the term 'cutting yourself off to Him' and clarifies that Islam explicitly rejects celibacy and monasticism, which are innovations not prescribed by Allah. The speaker emphasizes that marriage is integral to Islamic practice and faith, citing prophetic traditions against celibacy.
suggested_title: Tafsir Surah Al-Muzzammil 73:8-10 - Remembrance of Allah, Quranic Recitation Rules, and the Rejection of Celibacy in Islam
catchy_title: Why Islam Forbids Celibacy and Monasticism
